Outlier Detection¶

  • Outliers can be detected using a box-and-whisker plot.

    • In such plots, circles represent outliers.

  • The df.describe() function in pandas provides useful summary statistics:

    • min value → minimum data point
    • 25% (Q1) → first quartile (25% of the data lies below this value)
    • 50% (median) → middle value of the data
    • 75% (Q3) → third quartile (75% of the data lies below this value)
    • max value → maximum data point

  • Outliers are typically defined using the Interquartile Range (IQR) method:

    • IQR = Q3 − Q1
    • Lower bound = Q1 − 1.5 × IQR
    • Upper bound = Q3 + 1.5 × IQR
    • Any value less than the lower bound or greater than the upper bound is considered an outlier.
In [42]:
for col in df:
    plt.figure()
    df.boxplot([col])

/tmp/ipykernel_2379083/2216270953.py:2: RuntimeWarning: More than 20 figures have been opened. Figures created through the pyplot interface (`matplotlib.pyplot.figure`) are retained until explicitly closed and may consume too much memory. (To control this warning, see the rcParam `figure.max_open_warning`). Consider using `matplotlib.pyplot.close()`.
  plt.figure()
  • Eg: outlier example
  • Age : 20,22,25,28,900 # mistake due to human error (Here this outlier is a mistake since we cant have age 900).
  • Eg: Salary of a people in the room: A1: 50k, A2: 1l, Elonmusk: 100k So this outliers is not a mistake.
    • but regression models are sensitive to outliers.
    • We can use the mean value to replace the outliers if we have less outliers, In case of many outliers we can replace them with median values.
    • We can replace the missing values with mean value.

Correlation matrix:¶

In [47]:
corr_matrix = df.corr()
plt.figure(figsize=(20,20))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, cbar = True, fmt=".1f", annot= True, cmap="Blues")
plt.savefig("corr heat map")
plt.show()

Correlation Explanation¶

  • Correlation measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables.

  • Its value ranges from -1 to +1:

    • +1 → perfect positive relationship
    • -1 → perfect negative relationship
    • 0 → no linear relationship

  • Example:

    • If the correlation between X and Y is 0.3, this means there is a positive but weak linear relationship.
    • It does not mean that if X increases by 1, Y increases by exactly 0.3.
    • Instead, it means that on average, as X increases, Y also tends to increase, but the relationship is not very strong.

  • Important:

    • Correlation shows association, not causation.
    • A correlation of 0.3 only tells us the variables move together weakly in the same direction, not the exact change in values.

  • Advantage is : If we have high correlation between two features we can remove one of those in the feature selection part.

Advantage of Correlation in Feature Selection¶

  • One important use of correlation is in feature selection.
  • If two features have a high correlation with each other, they carry almost the same information.
  • In such cases, we can remove one of the features without losing much information.
  • This helps to:
    • Reduce redundancy in the dataset
    • Improve model performance
    • Prevent multicollinearity (especially in regression models)
    • Make the model simpler and easier to interpret

Multicollinearity¶

  • Multicollinearity occurs when two or more independent (predictor) variables in a dataset are highly correlated with each other.

  • This means that one predictor can be linearly predicted from another with a high degree of accuracy.

  • Why is it a problem?

    • It makes it difficult for models (especially regression models) to determine the individual effect of each predictor on the target variable.
    • Coefficients in regression become unstable and may change drastically with small changes in data.
    • It can lead to misleading conclusions and reduce the interpretability of the model.

  • How to detect multicollinearity?

    • Look at the correlation matrix of features.
    • Calculate Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) – a VIF value > 10 is often considered a sign of high multicollinearity.

  • How to handle it?

    • Remove one of the correlated features.
    • Use dimensionality reduction techniques such as PCA.
    • Regularization methods (e.g., Ridge or Lasso regression).

Inference:¶

  • Most of the features have outliers.
  • Some of the features have positive correlation, hence we can remove those.
